input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system studied is the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, with a particular focus on its first axis. The axis comprises a high‑torque motor, multi‑stage gearboxes, and flexible rotary joints that deliver high path repeatability. The robot is driven by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D SL controller and is employed in machining and other manufacturing tasks that require precise, repeatable motion.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real‑time monitoring of joint positions, velocities, and forces, continuously identifies and quantifies joint friction losses using multi‑physics‑aware models, and visualises these metrics in an integrated dashboard. It supports prediction of wear and degradation, enables optimisation of control parameters to improve accuracy, and offers closed‑loop assistance for trajectory planning and fault detection. The services target machine operators, control engineers, and maintenance personnel.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation relies on Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML for structural models and internal block diagrams, architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs to capture components, connectors, and composition, and behavior description via state machines or MontiArc Automaton. Simulation and detailed behavior modeling are performed with MathWorks MATLAB Simulink, while algorithmic implementation may use general‑purpose languages like C++ or Java. The approach integrates heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives through these heterogeneous modeling notations and toolchains.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin relies on a Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) stack. Structural and functional architectures are captured with SysML (including internal block diagrams) and refined through Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs. Behavioral aspects are expressed with SysML v1.7 state‑machine diagrams or with MontiArc Automaton automata. These abstract models are linked to concrete simulation artifacts, notably MATLAB/Simulink white‑box dynamic models, and may be complemented by data‑driven (black‑box) models such as machine‑learning predictors. The data side consists of contextual data traces—time‑stamped sensor measurements and aggregated abstractions—collected from the physical asset for a specific purpose. Data management includes aggregation, abstraction, and explicit traceability links that map each data artifact back to its source and to the model elements that consume it. Model relationships are expressed through verified transformations (e.g., SysML to Simulink) and composition of physics‑based and data‑driven components, enabling the DT to provide active reasoning and prediction capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is maintained through rigorous traceability of data artifacts and verification of model transformations. Each digital shadow data trace is linked to its generation process and to the corresponding model element, supporting impact analysis. Transformations from high‑level MBSE artifacts (e.g., SysML diagrams) to executable simulation models (e.g., Simulink) are subject to verification methods that check structural and semantic consistency. Validation is performed by comparing model outputs with the measured data traces provided by the digital shadow; observed deviations are used to assess accuracy and trigger model refinement. Sources of uncertainty such as sensor noise or modeling simplifications are acknowledged, and their impact is documented as part of the traceability information.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical asset (e.g., input current and output angle) are streamed to the digital representation using the MQTT publish/subscribe protocol. The raw measurements can be pre‑processed to derive torque, angular velocity, and acceleration, which are then aggregated as contextual data traces in accordance with the digital‑shadow reference model. The flow is automated and one‑way; triggers are based on measurement updates. No specific transmission frequency, data‑quality assurance procedures, or additional integration mechanisms are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between the physical sensors and the digital shadow uses the MQTT protocol (as referenced by the Mosquitto client/server implementation). The documents do not provide details on network topology, bandwidth or latency requirements, redundancy mechanisms, or security protocols (e.g., TLS) for this connection.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering and development of the digital twin follows a Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) approach. System architecture is captured using SysML and refined through a hierarchy of abstraction layers (requirements, functional, logical, product). Methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT are applied to manage complexity. Component‑connector models are expressed with ADLs (e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML internal block diagrams) and behavioral models may be realized with state machines, MontiArc Automaton or external simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink. The digital twin incorporates active processing components that consume input data traces and, using engineering models, simulation models or data‑calculation models, generate output traces that become part of the twin’s data shadow. Traceability from each output trace back to the originating processing component, input traces and involved models is maintained via the “originates‑from” association, supporting transitive provenance. Concrete realizations are obtained by mapping the abstract concepts of the Digital Shadow Reference Model to concrete class diagrams, establishing a conformance relation that guarantees that the instantiated twin conforms to the reference model.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The approach conforms to the SysML modeling language for system architecture and to UML class diagrams for the Digital Shadow Reference Model. The reference model defines a conformance relation that must be satisfied by any concrete realization, providing a standardised way to map domain concepts to concrete elements. ADLs such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc are employed as standard component‑connector description languages, and the processing step leverages standard simulation and data‑calculation model formats as defined by the reference model.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_

M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct

block_1_judge.reasoning:
The description is highly specific and accurately captures the details provided in Section III of the source text. It correctly identifies the robot model, specific axis focus, components (motor, gearboxes), controller, and application domain.

Supported by quotes:
- 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100'
- 'The first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow'
- 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'
- 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'

While the extraction correctly identifies services like friction identification and dashboard visualization, it includes significant unsupported claims that contradict the source text. The source explicitly defines the system as a 'digital shadow' which is 'passive' and 'neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back', whereas the extraction describes 'closed-loop assistance' and 'trajectory planning', implying active control. It also invents specific user roles not found in the text.

Supported quotes:
- 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction'
- 'visualization in a dashboard'
- 'a digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system'

The extraction provides a comprehensive list of tools and technologies mentioned in the source text. It correctly identifies Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML, mentions architecture description languages (ADLs) like ACME, AADL, and MontiArc, and cites simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink. The description aligns well with the background section (II.A) which lists these exact tools and their roles: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'architecture description languages (ADLs) are used... e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc... or SysML IBDs', and 'MathWorks Simulink [28]'. The extraction also correctly notes the use of general-purpose languages like C++ or Java, which are mentioned in the text ('code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java').

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

It correctly captures the distinction between white-box and black-box models, the definition of digital shadows as contextual data traces, and the emphasis on traceability and data management.

Supporting quotes:
- "To describe the functional and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used... ACME, AADL, MontiArc... or SysML IBDs"
- "Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink"
- "In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital shadow as 'a set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose'"
- "Both the models and the robot generate data that remains fully traceable to its origin and method of computation"

Supporting quotes:
- "The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor... a gear transmission is first used... followed by a cycloidal gear"
- "The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl"

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the sensing infrastructure, specifically the encoders used for position measurement and access to sensor data streams.

Supporting quotes:
- "There are two position encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox."
- "We have access to time series of all sensors... These are the input current and the output angle."

The extraction accurately reflects the text's approach to validation through the comparison of simulated and measured data and the reliance on traceability for model fidelity. It correctly identifies 'deviation' as the key metric for assessment and captures the concept of tracing data origins. It slightly generalizes the verification of transformations based on the text's discussion of conformance and traceability.

Supporting quotes:
- "solution scope of the deviation of simulated data versus measured data"
- "By following the originates from association, one can trace a data trace back to its generating processing component... and the models involved"
- "This traceability allows for a meaningful linking of all data"

block_5_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:
